name: environment
context: Understand and manage environment variables, PATH, shell configuration files, and process environments
info: Environment variables are key-value pairs that configure process behavior without modifying code. Every process inherits its parent's environment. This context covers how environment variables work, how to set and export them, the role of PATH in command resolution, dotfiles that configure shell sessions (.bashrc, .bash_profile, .profile, .env), the distinction between login and non-login shells, and tools for inspecting and manipulating the environment (env, printenv, export, unset). Agents need this knowledge to configure tools, manage secrets safely, and understand why commands succeed or fail based on PATH and variable state. See [[shell/bash]] for variable expansion syntax and [[shell/processes]] for how child processes inherit environment.
body:
# Environment Variables

Environment variables are the primary mechanism for configuring process behavior. They pass configuration from the shell to commands, scripts, and child processes without hardcoding values.

## Setting and Exporting Variables

```bash
# Shell variable (local to current shell, NOT inherited by children)
MY_VAR="value"

# Environment variable (exported to child processes)
export MY_VAR="value"

# Set and export in one line
export API_KEY="sk-abc123"

# Set for a single command only (does NOT persist)
DEBUG=true node server.js

# Unset a variable
unset MY_VAR

# Readonly variable (cannot be changed or unset)
readonly DB_HOST="localhost"
declare -r DB_HOST="localhost"  # Same thing
```

## Viewing the Environment

```bash
# All environment variables
env                          # POSIX standard
printenv                     # Same, slightly different output
export -p                    # Bash: all exported variables

# Specific variable
echo "$PATH"
printenv HOME
printf '%s\n' "$SHELL"

# All shell variables (including non-exported)
set                          # Everything: variables, functions
declare -p                   # All declared variables
```

## PATH — Command Resolution

PATH is a colon-separated list of directories. When you type a command, the shell searches PATH left-to-right for a matching executable.

```bash
# View PATH
echo "$PATH"
# /usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin

# Add to PATH (prepend — highest priority)
export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"

# Add to PATH (append — lowest priority)
export PATH="$PATH:/opt/custom/bin"

# Which executable will run?
which python          # First match in PATH
which -a python       # ALL matches in PATH
type -a python        # Same, plus builtins and aliases
command -v python     # POSIX-portable way to check

# Common PATH troubleshooting
# "command not found" means:
# 1. The program isn't installed, OR
# 2. Its directory isn't in PATH, OR
# 3. The file exists but lacks execute permission
```

## Key Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose | Example |
|----------|---------|----------|
| `PATH` | Executable search path | `/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin` |
| `HOME` | User home directory | `/home/user` |
| `USER` / `LOGNAME` | Current username | `developer` |
| `SHELL` | Login shell | `/bin/bash` |
| `PWD` | Current working directory | `/workspaces/project` |
| `OLDPWD` | Previous directory (`cd -`) | `/home/user` |
| `TERM` | Terminal type | `xterm-256color` |
| `LANG` / `LC_ALL` | Locale settings | `en_US.UTF-8` |
| `EDITOR` / `VISUAL` | Default editor | `vim`, `code --wait` |
| `TMPDIR` | Temporary file directory | `/tmp` |
| `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` | User config directory | `~/.config` |
| `XDG_DATA_HOME` | User data directory | `~/.local/share` |
| `HISTFILE` | Shell history file | `~/.bash_history` |
| `HISTSIZE` | History entries in memory | `1000` |
| `PS1` | Primary prompt string | `\u@\h:\w\$ ` |

## Shell Configuration Files

Shell startup files execute in a specific order depending on whether the shell is a login shell, interactive shell, or neither.

### Login Shell (ssh, `su -`, terminal login)

```
1. /etc/profile           (system-wide)
2. ~/.bash_profile        (user, checked first)
   OR ~/.bash_login       (fallback)
   OR ~/.profile          (fallback)
3. ~/.bash_logout         (on exit)
```

### Interactive Non-Login Shell (new terminal tab, subshell)

```
1. /etc/bash.bashrc       (system-wide, some distros)
2. ~/.bashrc              (user)
```

### Non-Interactive Shell (scripts, cron jobs)

```
1. $BASH_ENV              (if set, this file is sourced)
```

### Best Practice

Most users put everything in `~/.bashrc` and have `~/.bash_profile` source it:

```bash
# ~/.bash_profile
[[ -f ~/.bashrc ]] && source ~/.bashrc
```

## .env Files

Dotenv files store project-specific configuration. They are NOT automatically loaded by bash — you need to source them or use a dotenv library.

```bash
# .env file format
DATABASE_URL=postgres://localhost:5432/mydb
REDIS_URL=redis://localhost:6379
SECRET_KEY=supersecret123
DEBUG=true

# Load .env in bash (careful: no export prefix in file)
set -a                       # Auto-export all variables
source .env
set +a                       # Stop auto-exporting

# Alternative: export each line
export $(grep -v '^#' .env | xargs)

# Load specific variables
eval $(grep '^DATABASE_URL=' .env)
```

**Security**: NEVER commit `.env` files to git. Add `.env` to `.gitignore`. Use `.env.example` as a template with placeholder values.

## Process Environment Inheritance

Child processes inherit the parent's exported environment:

```bash
export FOO="bar"      # Set in parent
bash -c 'echo $FOO'  # Child sees "bar"

BAZ="qux"             # NOT exported
bash -c 'echo $BAZ'  # Child sees "" (empty)

# Override for single child
FOO="override" bash -c 'echo $FOO'   # "override"
echo "$FOO"                            # Still "bar" in parent
```

On Linux, inspect a running process's environment:
```bash
cat /proc/PID/environ | tr '\0' '\n'
```

## Environment Hygiene for Agents

```bash
# Check required variables before proceeding
: "${DATABASE_URL:?DATABASE_URL must be set}"
: "${API_KEY:?API_KEY must be set}"

# Use defaults for optional variables
PORT="${PORT:-8080}"
LOG_LEVEL="${LOG_LEVEL:-info}"

# Isolate environment for a subprocess
env -i HOME="$HOME" PATH="$PATH" command   # Clean environment

# Mask secrets in logs
echo "Using API key: ${API_KEY:0:4}..."
```

sections.title: PATH and command resolution
sections.body: PATH is a colon-separated list of directories searched left-to-right for executables. `which command` shows the first match. `which -a command` shows all matches. `type -a command` includes builtins and aliases. "command not found" means the executable isn't in PATH or doesn't have execute permission. Prepend to PATH for priority (`PATH="$HOME/bin:$PATH"`), append for fallback (`PATH="$PATH:/opt/bin"`). PATH changes in a script only affect that script's process unless exported and sourced.

sections.title: Shell configuration files
sections.body: Login shells source `~/.bash_profile` (or `~/.profile`). Interactive non-login shells source `~/.bashrc`. Best practice: put everything in `~/.bashrc` and have `~/.bash_profile` source it. System-wide config lives in `/etc/profile` and `/etc/bash.bashrc`. Non-interactive shells (scripts) source `$BASH_ENV` if set. Zsh uses `~/.zshrc` (interactive) and `~/.zprofile` (login). Changes to dotfiles take effect in new shells — use `source ~/.bashrc` to reload in current shell.

sections.title: Working with .env files
sections.body: .env files store project-specific environment variables. They are not loaded automatically by bash. Load with `set -a; source .env; set +a` (auto-export mode). Alternatively: `export $(grep -v '^#' .env | xargs)`. Never commit .env files — add to .gitignore, provide .env.example as a template. Node.js uses the `dotenv` package. Python uses `python-dotenv`. Docker Compose reads .env files natively. Format: `KEY=value` (no spaces around `=`, no export prefix).

sections.title: Environment inheritance
sections.body: Child processes inherit all exported variables from their parent. `export VAR=val` makes VAR available to children. Non-exported shell variables (`VAR=val`) are local. Override for a single command: `VAR=val command`. Process environment is copied at fork — subsequent parent changes don't propagate. On Linux, inspect `/proc/PID/environ` for a process's environment. Use `env -i` to start a process with a clean environment. `exec env` can be used to replace the current process with a modified environment.

links.context: Scripts use environment variables for configuration

related.note: Bash expansion syntax is how environment variables are accessed
Environment is inherited across process boundaries
Scripts rely on environment variables for external configuration

author: dojo-community
